original_hypothesis.title: AddressManager.getAddress is unscoped to caller context; cross-domain name collisions or incorrect chainId usage can misresolve critical dependencies
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: AddressManager stores addresses by (chainId,name). If any consumer incorrectly uses block.chainid vs configured chainId, or registers remote addresses under the wrong domain, critical dependencies (bridge, signal_service, verifiers) can be misresolved. While primarily a configuration risk, the simplicity of setAddress combined with broad dependency injection magnifies blast radius.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Many protocol security properties rely on resolve(name, required). A single incorrect mapping can redirect funds or verification to malicious contracts. This is not 'owner can do anything' but a concrete misconfiguration/upgrade hazard to validate across code paths.
deployment_assumptions: Owner/timelock mistakenly sets a wrong chainId domain or name mapping
Contracts resolve addresses for the wrong domain in AddressResolver (not shown in provided snippet)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: SetAddress.s.sol allows arbitrary DOMAIN/NAME/ADDRESS writes by owner; DeployOnL1.s.sol copies shared registrations into rollup address manager and registers L2 domain addresses separately, making domain mistakes plausible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: single mapping __addresses[chainId][name] set/get with no semantic validation of chainId/name beyond equality check

preconditions: A critical dependency is misregistered (wrong chainId or wrong name)
Resolve() in AddressResolver uses these mappings without additional validation (to be checked)
Attack manifests as fund routing or verification to wrong contract

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: dependency injection via simple key-value store
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: no guardrails preventing misregistration of core names
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no allowlist of names; no chainId sanity checks; no two-man rule on setAddress

analysis_questions: How does AddressResolver choose the domain chainId for lookups (block.chainid vs config.chainId vs stored)?
Which names are required=true in resolve() calls and what happens on address(0)?
Are there any on-chain invariants checking that resolved dependencies are contracts / correct codehash?
validation_requirements: Audit AddressResolver implementation and all resolve() call sites for domain selection
Review deployment scripts for domain usage consistency
Consider adding validation in resolve() or setAddress() for critical names

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A mistaken or compromised governance action registers an attacker-controlled 'tier_provider' or 'signal_service' under the active domain; subsequent propose/prove/verify calls use the malicious dependency to accept invalid proofs or stall verification.
prerequisites: Governance/admin mistake or compromise (off-chain), OR bug in domain resolution logic (on-chain)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potentially severe if malicious dependencies are injected (invalid proofs, fund misrouting), but depends on governance compromise/misconfig
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Fix via remapping/upgrade if detected in time

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No automated checks for correct domain/name wiring across deployments

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor AddressSet events for critical names and domains

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
